solution:
$\because \mathrm{A}$ is orthogonal matrix
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore A^T=A^{-1} \\ & \Rightarrow A^2=A^{-1} \left(\because \mathrm{A}^2=\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{T}}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow A^3=I \\ & \text { let } B=(A+I)^3+(A-I)^3-6 A \\ & =2\left(A^3+3 A\right)-6 A \\ & =2 A^3 \\ & B=2 I=\left[\begin{array}{lll}
2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2
\end{array}\right]
\end{aligned}$
Now sum of diagonal elements $=2+2+2=6$

subject: Mathematics
options: is a polynomial of degree 6 in $\mathrm{x}$.
is a polynomial of degree 3 in $\mathrm{x}$.
is a polynomial of degree 2 in $\mathrm{x}$.
solution:
Let $p_1 x=a_1 x^2+b_1 x+c_1$
$$
p_2 x=a_2 x^2+b_2 x+c_2
$$
and $p_3 x=a_3 x^2+b_3 x+c_3$
where $a_1, a_2, a_3, b_1, b_2, b_3, c_1, c_2, c_3$ are real numbers.
$$
\begin{gathered}
\therefore \mathrm{A}(x)=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
a_1 x^2+b_1 x+c_1 & 2 a_1 x+b_1 & 2 a_1 \\
a_2 x^2+b_2 x+c_2 & 2 a_2 x+b_2 & 2 a_2 \\
a_3 x^2+b_3 x+c_3 & 2 a_3 x+b_3 & 2 a_3
\end{array}\right] \\
=\left[\begin{array}{llll}
a_1 x^2+b_1 x+c_1 & a_2 x^2+b_2 x+c_2 & a_3 x^2+b_3 x+c_3 \\
2 a_1 x+b_1 & 2 a_2 x+b_2 & 2 a_3 x+b_2 \\
2 a_1 & 2 a_2 & 2 a_3
\end{array}\right] \\
\times\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
a_1 x^2+b_1 x+c_1 & 2 a_1 x+b_1 & 2 a_1 \\
a_2 x^2+b_2 x+c_2 & 2 a_2 x+b_2 & 2 a_2 \\
a_3 x^2+b_3 x+c_3 & 2 a_3 x+b_3 & 2 a_3
\end{array}\right]
\end{gathered}
$$
It is clear from the above multiplication, the degree of determinant of $\mathrm{B}(x)$ can not be less than 4 .

=C310-16=104    ⋯3

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & x \\ 3 & -1 & 2\end{array}\right]$ and $\mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{l}y \\ x \\ 1\end{array}\right]$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\mathrm{AB}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & 2 & x \\
3 & -1 & 2
\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l}
y \\
x \\
1
\end{array}\right] \\
&\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l}
6 \\
8
\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}
y+2 x+x \\
3 y-x+2
\end{array}\right] \\
&\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l}
6 \\
8
\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}
y+3 x \\
3 y-x+2
\end{array}\right] \\
&\Rightarrow y+3 x=6 \text { and } 3 y-x=6
\end{aligned}
$$
On solving, we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
&x=\frac{6}{5} \text { and } y=\frac{12}{5} \\
&\Rightarrow y=2 x
\end{aligned}
$$

$$

&x^2-6 y \\
&2 x^2-4 y^2=9
\end{aligned}
$$
Consider the line,
$$
x-y=\frac{3}{2}
$$
On solving (i) and (iii), we get only
$$
x=3, y=\frac{3}{2}
$$
Hence $\left(3, \frac{3}{2}\right)$ is the point of contact of conic (i), and line (iii)
On solving (ii) and (iii), we get only $x=3$,
$$
y=\frac{3}{2}
$$
Hence $\left(3, \frac{3}{2}\right)$ is also the point of contact of conic (ii) and line (iii).
Hence line (iii) is the common tangent to both the given conics.

$x^2+x+1=0$
$\alpha$ is root
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \alpha^2+\alpha+1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=\omega \text { as } \omega^2 \text { [cube root of unity] }
\end{aligned}$
also
$\left.\begin{array}{l}
{\left[\begin{array}{ll}
4-a-2 b & 64-a-14 b
\end{array} 52+2 a-8 b\right.}
\end{array}\right]$
$\begin{aligned} & =\left[\begin{array}{ll}0 & 0&0\end{array}\right] \\ \therefore & a+2 b=4 \\ & a+14 b=64 \\ \Rightarrow & 12 b=60 \Rightarrow b=5 \\ \Rightarrow & a=-6 \\ \therefore & \frac{4}{\alpha^4}+\frac{m}{\alpha^{-6}}+\frac{n}{\alpha^5}=3 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{4}{\omega}+\frac{m}{1}+\frac{n}{\omega^2}=3 \\ \Rightarrow & 4 \omega^2+m+n \omega=3\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 4\left(-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{i}\right)+\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}\left(-\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{i}\right)=3 \\ & \therefore-2+\mathrm{m}-\frac{\mathrm{n}}{2}=3....(1) \\ & \& \frac{-4 \sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{n} \sqrt{3}}{2}=0 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{n}=4 \\ & \mathrm{~m}=7 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{n}=11
\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The determinant of matrix $A$ is given by:

$|A| = 1(0 - 3) - 1(-10 - 1) + 2(-6 - 0)$

$|A| = -3 + 11 - 12 = -4$

We know the property $A(\text{adj}A) = |A|I$, which gives $\text{adj}A = |A|A^{-1}$.

Taking the inverse on both sides:

$(\text{adj}A)^{-1} = \dfrac{A}{|A|} = \dfrac{A}{-4}$

Let $B = 2(\text{adj}A)^{-1}$. Substituting the above expression, we get:

$B = 2\left(\dfrac{A}{-4}\right) = -\dfrac{1}{2}A$

We need to find the matrix $\text{adj}(\text{adj}B)$. For a square matrix of order $n$, we have $\text{adj}(\text{adj}B) = |B|^{n-2}B$. Since the order is $n = 3$:

$\text{adj}(\text{adj}B) = |B|^{3-2}B = |B|B$

Now, we calculate the determinant of $B$:

$|B| = \left|-\dfrac{1}{2}A\right| = \left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^3|A| = -\dfrac{1}{8}(-4) = \dfrac{1}{2}$

Substituting $|B|$ back into the expression for $\text{adj}(\text{adj}B)$:

$\text{adj}(\text{adj}B) = \dfrac{1}{2}B = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}A\right) = -\dfrac{1}{4}A$

The sum of all elements of matrix $A$ is:

$1 + 1 + 2 - 2 + 0 + 1 + 1 + 3 + 5 = 12$

Therefore, the sum of all elements of the matrix $-\dfrac{1}{4}A$ is:

$-\dfrac{1}{4} \times 12 = -3$

Answer: $-3$

solution:
Given,

PT=aP+(a-1)I......(1)

Taking transpose on both sides we get,

PTT=aP+(a-1)IT

⇒P=aPT+(a-1)I.......(2)

Subtract equation (1) and (2),

⇒PT-P=aP-PT

⇒P=PT as a≠-1

From equation (1),

P=aP+(a-1)I

⇒P=-I

Taking adjoint on both sides,

⇒Adj P=Adj(-I)

⇒Adj P=Adj (-I)=-I2

⇒|AdjP|=1
